title: Stories (Ephemeral Content) — System Design
description: Design ephemeral stories: short-lived posts (photo/video) that appear in followers' story trays and auto-vanish after 24 hours. The themes are TTL-driven expiry everywhere, fanout to a per-user tray of unexpired stories, per-viewer seen-state, and cheap automatic cleanup at scale.

nodes.label: Mobile Client
nodes.description: Posts stories, views followees' story trays, marks them seen.

nodes.label: API Gateway
nodes.description: Routes post, tray, and view requests.

nodes.label: Story Post Service
nodes.description: Accepts a story, stores media, sets TTL, fans out to followers.

nodes.label: Story Store
nodes.description: Story metadata with expiry, sharded by author; auto-purged.

nodes.label: Social Graph
nodes.description: Follower edges plus audience state (close-friends membership, blocks, hidden-from) for fanout-time eligibility and read-time revalidation.

nodes.label: Tray Read Service
nodes.description: Server-side tray-read path: authenticates the viewer, reads the tray cache, filters expired entries, applies seen-state, merges celebrity-pull stories, and revalidates audience against current relationship state.

nodes.label: Fanout Queue
nodes.description: Buffers fanout jobs so a popular post doesn't block; scalable workers drain it.

nodes.label: Fanout Worker
nodes.description: Drains the fanout queue and pushes a new story id (idempotently) into each follower's story tray.

nodes.label: Story Tray Cache
nodes.description: Per-user list of unexpired stories from followees (with TTL).

nodes.label: Seen-State Store
nodes.description: Which stories a user has already viewed (per viewer).

nodes.label: Expiry Reaper
nodes.description: Purges expired stories from stores/trays past their TTL.

nodes.label: View Service
nodes.description: Authenticates a view, checks expiry + audience visibility, dedupes by (viewer, story), marks seen, and emits a view event. Also serves the author-facing viewer-list read after checking the caller owns the story.

nodes.label: View Event Stream
nodes.description: View events for seen-state, view counts, and analytics.

nodes.label: View Aggregator
nodes.description: Consumes view events and builds per-story viewer lists and counts (exact small / HLL at celebrity scale).

nodes.label: Viewer List / Count Store
nodes.description: Per-story viewer list (capped/paginated) and view count, TTL-aligned to the story.

nodes.label: Observability
nodes.description: Fanout queue lag, tray read p99, stale-filter drops, TTL purge lag, view aggregation lag, CDN expired-media rate.

links.label: read viewer list + count (after author check)

functional: Post a story (photo/video) visible to followers for a limited time (e.g. 24h).
View followees' active stories as a tray, ordered (often unseen-first).
Track which stories a viewer has already seen.
Show the author who viewed their story (viewer list / counts).
Automatically remove stories when they expire.

Respect a basic audience policy: public-to-followers vs. a close-friends subset, plus blocks / hidden-from. (Full private-account approval flows are out of scope; the relevant checks are called out at fanout vs. read time.)
nonFunctional: Everything is ephemeral — expiry must be reliable and cheap (no expired story ever shown).
Read-heavy: viewing trays vastly outnumbers posting.
Low-latency tray reads and media playback (CDN).
Per-viewer seen-state at scale (each viewer x each story).
Scale to large follower counts; cleanup must not be a manual or expensive sweep.

capacity.label: Follower fanout
capacity.note: 6k posts/s × 200 ≈ ~1.2M tray writes/s average — heavy-tailed, so celebrities use pull.

capacity.label: Tray opens
capacity.note: Read-heavy; each open is a cached tray lookup + seen filter.

capacity.label: View events
capacity.note: Each opened story emits a view → must be async + deduped, never a synchronous row write.

capacity.label: Live data
capacity.note: ~500M stories × ~1MB media ≈ ~0.5 PB live media; metadata ~few hundred bytes/story. All auto-expires.

capacity.label: Fanout lag budget
capacity.note: A normal post should land in followers' trays within seconds; queue backlog beyond that degrades tray UX.

capacity.label: Tray-entry writes & memory
capacity.note: 6k posts/s × ~200 push followers ≈ ~1.2M tray writes/s (celebrities excluded via pull). Each entry is ~tens of bytes (story_id + expiresAt + author). Live tray entries ≈ DAU × avg active followee-stories; at ~tens of bytes each this is ~hundreds of GB — sharded by viewer, so spread it wide.

capacity.label: Seen-state live entries
capacity.note: Bounded by the 24h window: one membership entry per story a viewer actually opens. At ~16–24 bytes/entry, even billions of opens/day stay in the low-TB range and auto-expire — sharded by viewer.

capacity.label: View-event stream
capacity.note: At ~40–60 bytes/event (story_id, viewer_id, ts, idem key) → ~hundreds of MB/s. Partition by story_id so celebrity spikes (10M+/story) spread across consumers; retain a short replay window (e.g. hours) for aggregator recovery — not 24h.

capacity.label: Viewer-store size
capacity.value: exact lists for ordinary, HLL for celebrity
capacity.note: Ordinary stories keep an exact capped/paginated list (~bytes × viewers). Celebrity stories cap the list and switch counts to a fixed-size HLL sketch (~1.5–12 KB/story regardless of 10M+ views), so a viral story can't blow up memory.

capacity.label: Media bandwidth
capacity.note: ~500M × ~1MB ≈ ~0.5 PB/day uploaded; transcoding to a few renditions + thumbnail multiplies stored bytes ~2–4×. Reads are far larger but CDN-served — with a high edge hit ratio, origin (MediaStore) egress is a small fraction of total view bandwidth.

capacity.label: Rebuild-rate budget
capacity.value: throttled to a fraction of normal read QPS
capacity.note: On a tray-cache shard loss, rebuilds (fan-in over followees' active stories) are rate-limited/jittered to a bounded fraction of story-store read capacity so a mass eviction doesn't stampede the source of truth; a partial tray is served while warming.

api.description: Post a story; stores media with a server-assigned TTL and enqueues fanout to followers' trays. expiresAt is set by the server (not the client) at create time.
Get the viewer's story tray (active stories from followees, unseen-first).
Record a view: auth + expiry/visibility check, idempotent mark-seen by (viewer, story), and an emitted view event for async viewer-list/count aggregation.

messages.label: load story: not expired, status=active, audience

api.description: Author reads who viewed their story (capped/paginated list + count) from the async-built viewer store. The view service verifies the caller owns the story before returning the list; ordering is by first view (earliest viewer first), with recency available as a secondary sort.

messages.label: read capped/paginated list + count (ordered by first view)

dataModel.name: stories
dataModel.note: Story metadata with an expiry and its audience policy; auto-purged, sharded by author.

fields.name: audience_snapshot_version
fields.type: bigint (graph version at fanout; lets reads detect stale eligibility)

fields.name: status
fields.type: enum (active | taken_down) — set on report/policy block before TTL

dataModel.name: story_tray (cache)
dataModel.note: Per-user list of unexpired story ids from followees, with TTL entries.

dataModel.name: seen_state (cache)
dataModel.note: Which stories a viewer has seen (ephemeral, TTL-aligned).

dataModel.name: view_events (stream)
dataModel.note: Append-only view events; consumed by the aggregator. Deduped by (viewer_id, story_id) so client retries don't double-count.

dataModel.name: story_viewers (cache)
dataModel.note: Per-story viewer list + count, built async and TTL-aligned to the story. List capped/paginated; count exact for small, HLL estimate at celebrity scale.

patterns.name: TTL-native storage
patterns.what: Tag every story/media/tray entry with an expiry so the store auto-purges it (no manual delete).
patterns.whenToUse: Content that is meant to disappear on a deadline.

patterns.name: Fanout-on-write to trays
patterns.what: Push a new story id into each follower's tray at post time for cheap reads.
patterns.whenToUse: Read-heavy ephemeral feeds with bounded fanout.

patterns.name: Hybrid fanout (celebrity pull)
patterns.what: Pull stories of very-high-follower authors at read time instead of pushing.

patterns.name: Audience policy with read-time revalidation
patterns.what: Store each story's audience (followers/close-friends) and check eligibility at fanout time, then revalidate at read/view time against current relationship state so unfollow/block/close-friends changes during the live window suppress already-fanned-out entries.
patterns.whenToUse: Time-limited content whose audience can change while it is still visible.

patterns.name: Per-viewer seen-state
patterns.what: Track seen stories per viewer to order unseen-first and dim seen ones.
patterns.whenToUse: Story UIs that distinguish new vs viewed.

patterns.name: Lazy + TTL expiry
patterns.what: Rely on store TTL for cleanup plus filter expired entries on read; a reaper backstops trays.
patterns.group: Reliable Expiry (Never Show a Stale Story)

patterns.name: Async view counting
patterns.what: Aggregate view events for the author's viewer list/counts off the hot path, deduped by (viewer, story).

patterns.name: Bounded async fanout
patterns.what: Buffer fanout jobs in a queue drained by autoscaled workers with idempotent tray writes and a lag budget.
patterns.whenToUse: Bursty fanout with heavy-tailed follower counts.

patterns.name: Rebuildable derived state
patterns.what: Treat trays/seen-state/viewer-lists as caches rebuildable (rate-limited) from the authoritative self-expiring store.
patterns.whenToUse: Derived read stores that can be reconstructed from a source of truth.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Normal Posts + Nightly Delete + Assemble-on-Read
description: Start with the simplest thing that works. Store a story exactly like a permanent post — a row in the story store and a media blob — with no special lifecycle. To make it 'ephemeral', run a nightly batch job (the reaper) that scans the whole store and deletes anything older than 24h. To show a tray, query each followee on read for their recent, not-yet-deleted stories and merge them.

This is correct in spirit and dead simple — and it exposes the three problems the rest of the design solves. First, a nightly scan-and-delete over hundreds of millions of rows is brutally expensive, and between runs the store holds far more than a day of data. Second, until that job runs, expired stories are still present, so a naive read can show content that should already be gone. Third, assembling a tray by querying every followee on each read is far too slow for a read-heavy story feed.

So the baseline motivates everything that follows: make expiry intrinsic (TTL-native storage that auto-purges), precompute trays with fanout, and always filter expired entries on read so a stale story is never shown.

view.caption: The mobile client posts through the gateway to the post service, which stores media and the story while a reaper purges expired rows from the story store.

steps.decisionPrompt: If you store stories like normal posts and delete them with a nightly scan, how much extra data sits around, and what stops an expired story from being shown before the job runs?
whyNow: Establishing the naive baseline first makes it obvious why expiry must be intrinsic (TTL) rather than a scan job, why trays must be precomputed, and why reads must filter expired entries — each later step removes a specific pain from here.

recap.after: Stories stored like permanent posts; a nightly job scan-deletes expired ones; trays assembled by querying followees on read.
recap.newRisk: Three debts: stale visibility (expired stories shown before the job runs), read fan-in cost (per-read tray assembly is too slow), and unbounded storage (far more than 24h of data sits around between sweeps).

traps.trap: Treating stories as normal posts and 'expiring' them with a scheduled scan-delete.
traps.why: Scanning hundreds of millions of rows daily is expensive, the store holds far more than 24h of data, and any lag lets an expired story be shown.
traps.instead: TTL-native storage that auto-purges, precomputed trays via fanout, and filter-on-read so no expired story is ever shown.

steps.title: 2. Ephemeral by Design: TTL Everywhere
description: The defining property is that stories vanish after a deadline. So make expiry intrinsic, not a bolted-on cleanup job: every artifact — the story record, its media, and tray entries — carries an expiresAt and is stored in TTL-native stores that auto-purge.

A story is posted with media stored in an object store tagged with a lifecycle/TTL rule, and a story record whose store auto-expires rows past expires_at. Nothing requires a scan-and-delete job to enumerate and remove old content.

Media itself goes through a small pipeline before it's live: the client uploads to a pre-signed URL (so bytes don't transit the post service), the media is transcoded to playback renditions and a thumbnail, and a safety scan runs; only then is the story made visible. Each rendition inherits the same lifecycle/TTL so the whole media set expires together.
This 'TTL-native' choice is the foundation: it makes cleanup cheap and automatic, which is essential when hundreds of millions of stories are created and must all disappear daily.

One caveat to carry forward: TTL purge is not precise physical deletion. Purge timing is approximate, and backups, replicas, CDN caches, and analytics logs may retain data briefly past expiry. The user-facing guarantee — 'never shown after expiry' — comes later from read-time filtering and bounded media authorization (Step 5), not from the purge itself.

view.caption: The mobile client posts through the gateway to the post service, which writes media to the object store and the story to the story store, each tagged with a TTL.

options.name: TTL-native stores (auto-purge) (default)
options.description: Every artifact (story record, media object, tray entry) carries an expiresAt and lives in a store that auto-expires it, so cleanup is built into the storage layer.
pros: Every artifact (story record, media, tray entry) carries expiresAt and lives in a store that auto-expires it.
No scan-and-delete job to enumerate hundreds of millions of expired stories a day.
Cleanup cost is built into the storage layer, so it scales automatically with volume.
cons: TTL purge timing is approximate, so an expired id can linger briefly (handled by read-time filtering later).
Requires stores that natively support per-row/object TTL.
Media object TTL and record TTL must be aligned or one outlives the other.

view.caption: The post service writes to TTL-native media and story stores that auto-purge expired entries with no separate cleanup job.

options.name: Scheduled reaper sweep (batch delete)
options.description: A periodic reaper job scans the stores for expired stories and batch-deletes them, working even on stores without native per-row TTL.
pros: Works on any store, even ones without native TTL support.
Centralized deletion logic that's easy to reason about and audit.
Can batch deletes for efficiency on the storage engine.
cons: A periodic scan over hundreds of millions of rows is brutally expensive at this scale.
Between runs, expired stories sit around and could be served — exactly the bug to avoid.
Delete throughput must keep up with posting rate or the backlog grows.

view.caption: The post service writes stories and media with expiry, and a scheduled reaper sweeps both stores to batch-delete expired entries.

options.name: Lazy delete-on-read
options.description: Expired data is never deleted proactively; it is simply ignored and removed only when a read next touches it.
pros: Nothing is deleted proactively — expired data is simply ignored/removed when next touched.
Zero background cost for stories no one ever reads again.
Read-time check already needed as a safety net, so this reuses it.
cons: Cold data that is never read again is never reclaimed — storage grows unbounded.
Bounded ~24h live-data advantage is lost if junk lingers indefinitely.
Still needs some background sweep to actually reclaim space.

view.caption: The client reads the tray, filtering and dropping expired stories on read while the tray lazily purges the story store on touch.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you delete hundreds of millions of expired stories a day without an expensive sweep job?

concepts.definition: Every artifact carries an expiry; stores auto-purge past it (object lifecycle rules, DB TTL).
concepts.whyItMatters: Makes ephemeral cleanup automatic and cheap instead of a giant scheduled delete.
concepts.example: Story row expires at created+24h; media lifecycle rule deletes the blob.

whyNow: TTL-native storage is the foundational decision for ephemeral content; it's what makes the whole 'disappears at scale' requirement tractable.

recap.newRisk: Followers need to see new stories fast — a read-heavy fanout problem like any feed.

steps.title: 3. Fanout to Story Trays
description: Viewing is read-heavy, so precompute each user's story tray: when someone posts, fan the story id out into each follower's tray cache (fanout-on-write), so reading the tray is a cheap cached lookup rather than a query across all followees.

Fanout is async: POST /v1/stories returns as soon as the story is durably written and a fanout job is enqueued; workers then drain the queue. A normal-author fanout finishing in seconds is fine — followers see the story land in their tray within the lag budget. Tray writes are idempotent (push keyed by (follower, story) id) so retries after a worker crash don't duplicate entries.

Audience policy is concrete here. Each story carries an audience (followers or close_friends) plus an audience_snapshot_version (the graph version at fanout time). At fanout the worker reads the social graph and pushes only into eligible followers' trays: close-friends membership is honored, and blocked / hidden-from viewers are excluded. But the audience is a contract that must hold for the full 24h window, and relationships change inside it: a viewer who unfollows, gets blocked, or is dropped from close-friends after fanout still holds a now-stale tray entry. So eligibility is rechecked at read time — both the tray read and view recording revalidate against current relationship state via the graph, and the snapshot version lets the read path spot entries built under an older audience. Fanout-time filtering keeps the common case cheap; read-time revalidation is the correctness backstop — the same filter-on-read discipline used for expiry, now applied to audience.

Tray entries themselves carry the story's TTL, so the tray naturally only contains active stories. As with any feed, the heavy-tailed follower distribution means celebrities are handled by pull (fetch their active stories at read time) rather than pushing to tens of millions of trays — a hybrid fanout.
The tray stores story ids (and authors); media is hydrated from the CDN on view. This is the same fanout model as a permanent feed, but every entry is short-lived.

view.caption: The post service enqueues a fanout job; fanout workers drain the queue, read followers from the social graph, and push the story id into each follower's tray cache.

options.name: Hybrid fanout: push for most, pull for celebrities (default)
options.description: Posting fans the story id into each follower's tray cache, except for celebrity authors whose active stories are pulled at read time and merged into the tray.
pros: Fanout-on-write makes the common tray read a cheap cached lookup, not a query across all followees.
Celebrity authors with tens of millions of followers are pulled at read time, avoiding mega-fanout.
Tray entries carry the story TTL, so the tray naturally holds only active stories.
Fanout runs async off a queue with idempotent tray writes, so the post returns fast and worker retries don't duplicate entries.
cons: Two code paths (push and pull) to build, tune, and reconcile at read time.
Choosing the celebrity threshold is a tuning knob with edge cases.
Merging pushed tray entries with pulled celebrity stories adds read-time complexity.

view.caption: Posts enqueue a fanout job; workers drain the queue and push story ids to most followers' tray caches via the social graph, leaving celebrity stories to be pulled at read.

options.name: Pure fanout-on-write (push to every tray)
options.description: Every post writes the story id into every follower's tray, even a celebrity's tens of millions, so a tray read is always a single cached lookup.
pros: Reading a tray is always a single cheap cached lookup — no read-time merge.
Simplest read path: trays are fully materialized for everyone.
Predictable, low read latency regardless of who you follow.
cons: A 50M-follower post triggers 50M tray writes — a write storm that delays the post and wastes work.
Most of those trays may never be opened within the 24h window.
Hot authors make write amplification the dominant cost.

view.caption: Posting pushes the story id directly into every follower's tray cache (no queue), reading the full follower list from the social graph.

options.name: Pure fanout-on-read (pull at tray open)
options.description: Posting just writes the story; opening a tray queries all of that viewer's followees for their active stories and merges them on the fly.
pros: Posting is cheap: just write the story, no tray writes at all.
No wasted work for stories never viewed by a given follower.
No tray cache to keep consistent with posts.
cons: Every tray open queries all followees' active stories — expensive for users following thousands.
Read latency spikes exactly when traffic is highest (viewing is read-heavy).
Repeated tray opens redo the same fan-in query.

view.caption: At tray open the client reads who it follows from the social graph and pulls their active stories straight from the story store.

steps.decisionPrompt: A user with 50M followers posts a story. Do you push it into 50M trays?

concepts.definition: Push a story id into followers' trays at post time; entries expire with the story.
concepts.whyItMatters: Cheap tray reads, and the tray self-cleans as entries expire.
concepts.example: Push to normal followers' trays; pull celebrities at read time.

whyNow: Fanout is the read-scaling mechanism; reusing the push/pull hybrid with TTL'd entries connects it to feeds while staying ephemeral.

recap.after: Per-user trays of active stories via hybrid fanout.
recap.newRisk: Users want to know which stories are new — per-viewer seen-state.

flows.name: Post → hybrid tray fanout
flows.note: On post, enqueue a fanout job; workers push the story id (with its TTL) idempotently into normal followers' trays; very-high-follower authors are pulled at read time instead.

messages.label: push story id (TTL entry, idempotent) into each tray
no push — pulled at read time

steps.title: 4. Per-Viewer Seen-State
description: Story UIs distinguish unseen (bright ring, shown first) from already-viewed. That requires tracking, per viewer, which stories they've seen — a viewer x story relationship that's large but, helpfully, also ephemeral (it only matters while the story is alive).

Keep seen-state in a fast store keyed by viewer (a set of seen story ids), aligned to the story TTL so it auto-expires too. Reading the tray filters/orders by this set (unseen-first); viewing a story adds it to the set.

Seen-state is a service-owned store, not a client-addressable surface. The mark-seen write belongs to the view service (Step 6): it authenticates the viewer, checks the story is unexpired and the viewer is still eligible, and writes idempotently — so retries don't corrupt state and a viewer can't forge a seen mark. On the read side, the tray read service consults the seen-set to order unseen-first. Either way the client talks to a service, never to the cache directly.
Because seen-state expires with the stories, it never accumulates unbounded — the ephemerality that defines stories also bounds this otherwise-explosive relationship.

view.caption: The view service owns the idempotent mark-seen write to the seen-state store; the tray read service reads that store to order the tray unseen-first. The cache is never client-addressable.

options.name: Per-viewer seen-set keyed by viewer (default)
options.description: Each viewer has a set of seen story ids, aligned to the story TTL, so unseen-first ordering is a membership check and the set auto-expires with the stories.
pros: A set of seen story ids per viewer makes 'unseen-first' ordering a fast membership check.
Aligned to the story TTL, so seen-state auto-expires and never grows unbounded.
Marking seen on view is a cheap add to the viewer's set.
cons: Storage is viewer x active-stories, large but bounded by the 24h window.
Sharding by viewer means an author's 'who saw mine' is a different access pattern.
Hot viewers (huge followee counts) have big sets to read on tray open.

view.caption: The view service writes a per-viewer seen-set keyed by viewer; the tray read service reads it to order unseen-first.

options.name: Per-story viewer bitmap / set
options.description: Seen-state is keyed by story as a set or bitmap of viewers, so the author's viewer list comes straight from one structure.
pros: Keyed by story, so the author's viewer list and count come straight from one structure.
Compact for a story with a bounded, known viewer universe (e.g. a bitmap over follower index).
Natural fit when the dominant query is 'who viewed this story'.
cons: The reader's 'which of my followees' stories have I seen' becomes a scatter-read across many stories.
A celebrity story's viewer set is enormous — millions of entries per story.
Bitmaps need a stable viewer->index mapping that's awkward across a changing follower graph.

view.caption: Seen-state keyed by story as a per-story viewer set, written by the view service and read by the tray read service.

options.name: High-water-mark timestamp per (viewer, author)
options.description: Instead of a set of ids, store one 'last seen at' timestamp per viewer/author pair and treat anything older than the mark as already seen.
pros: Store just one 'last seen at' timestamp per viewer/author pair instead of a set of ids.
Tiny storage: anything older than the mark is treated as seen.
Cheap to update — bump the timestamp on view.
cons: Can't represent 'seen story B but not the older story A' — ordering assumptions may be wrong.
A late-arriving older story (or out-of-order posting) is incorrectly marked seen.
Loses exact per-story granularity the UI may want.

view.caption: Seen-state as a high-water-mark timestamp per (viewer, author), written by the view service and read by the tray read service.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you track which of a user's many stories each of their many followers has seen, without it growing forever?

concepts.definition: Per-viewer set of seen story ids, TTL-aligned to the stories so it self-bounds.
concepts.whyItMatters: Enables unseen-first UX without an ever-growing viewer x story table.
concepts.example: seen[viewer] = {s_9, s_42}; expires as those stories do.

whyNow: Seen-state is the story-specific UX requirement; recognizing it's bounded by the same TTL is the elegant insight.

recap.newRisk: Even with TTLs, expired entries can linger in caches — reads must never show an expired story.

steps.title: 5. Reliable Expiry (Never Show a Stale Story)
description: TTL stores purge eventually, but purge timing is approximate — an expired story id could still sit in a tray cache for a bit. The correctness boundary is read authorization, not cleanup: the server assigns an authoritative expiresAt at create time, tray entries carry that expiresAt, and tray reads filter out any entry past it before returning. That read-time filter — not the reaper — is what guarantees no expired story is ever shown.

Media must expire on the same boundary: a story's media is served via signed/CDN-authorized URLs whose validity is capped to the story's expiry, so a stale story can't be fetched through an old URL even if the object lingers in the store or a CDN edge cache. Object-store lifecycle and DB TTL may lag without violating the user-facing guarantee.

The reaper is only a cheap backstop: it drops straggler tray ids and reconciles orphaned media in a pass over recently-touched data, never a global scan. Product ephemerality means 'not shown after expiry', not instant physical erasure from every replica, CDN cache, backup, and analytics log.

The same read-time gate also handles early removal: if a story is reported or policy-blocked, its status flips to taken_down and reads treat it exactly like an expired entry — dropped on read, signed URLs revoked, CDN invalidated — so a story can disappear before its TTL with no special deletion path. (Pre-publish safety scanning lives in Step 2; this is the after-the-fact case.)
Layering read-time filtering and bounded media authorization over TTL purge gives both correctness (read filter) and cheap cleanup (TTL) — the key to ephemeral correctness at scale.

view.caption: Read-time filtering on the tray is the guarantee that stale stories never appear; the expiry reaper is a cheap backstop that purges the story store and drops straggler ids from the tray cache.

options.name: Read-time filter over TTL purge (default)
options.description: Reads drop any tray entry whose expiresAt has passed as an always-correct safety net, while TTL auto-purge does the bulk background cleanup.
pros: Read-time filtering drops any entry whose expiresAt has passed — an always-correct safety net regardless of purge lag.
TTL auto-purge does the bulk cleanup cheaply in the background.
Guarantees no expired story is ever shown even though purge timing is approximate.
cons: Every read pays a small filter cost on each tray entry.
A lightweight reaper is still wanted to drop straggler ids and reconcile media.
Two mechanisms (filter + purge) to reason about together.

view.caption: Reads filter expired entries as the always-correct guarantee; the reaper purges the story store and drops straggler tray ids as cheap background cleanup.

options.name: Rely on TTL purge alone
options.description: Reads do no expiry checks and the stores simply expire data on their own TTL schedule, with no read-time filter or reaper.
pros: Simplest: no read-time filtering, the stores just expire data.
No reaper to run — purge is the storage layer's job.
Cheapest read path since reads do no expiry checks.
cons: Purge timing is approximate, so an expired story can be served for seconds — the exact defect to avoid.
Different stores (record, media, tray) purge at different times, exposing inconsistencies.
No safety net when a purge is delayed under load.

view.caption: The system relies on approximate TTL purge alone to expire entries from the story store, tray cache, and media store.

options.name: Aggressive reaper as primary deleter
options.description: An active reaper sweeps all stores frequently to delete expired stories promptly as the primary deletion mechanism.
pros: An active reaper deletes expired stories promptly across all stores, minimizing the lingering window.
Single place enforces the expiry guarantee, easy to audit.
Works even on stores without native TTL.
cons: A frequent sweep over hundreds of millions of stories is expensive — the very cost TTL-native design avoids.
Still a race window between expiry and the reaper run unless reads also filter.
Reaper throughput must keep pace with posting volume or backlog builds.

view.caption: An aggressive reaper is the primary deleter, purging the story store, tray cache, and seen-state store on expiry.

steps.decisionPrompt: A story expired 30 seconds ago but its id is still in some trays. How do you guarantee no one sees it?

concepts.definition: Always drop expired entries at read time; rely on store TTL for bulk cleanup; a reaper backstops.
concepts.whyItMatters: Guarantees correctness (read filter) while keeping cleanup cheap (TTL).
concepts.example: Tray read skips entries past expiresAt even before TTL purge runs.
concepts.group: Reliable Expiry (Never Show a Stale Story)

whyNow: Reliable expiry is the correctness crux of ephemeral content; filter-on-read over TTL purge is the layered answer that never shows a stale story.

recap.before: Approximate purge could leak a stale story.
recap.after: Read-time filtering guarantees no expired story shown.
recap.newRisk: Authors want to know who viewed their story — high-volume view tracking.

traps.trap: Relying only on a scheduled deletion job to remove expired stories.
traps.why: Scanning hundreds of millions daily is expensive, and any lag risks showing expired content.
traps.instead: TTL-native stores + filter-on-read; reaper only as a cheap backstop.

flows.name: Tray read: filter-on-read never shows a stale story
flows.note: The tray read service is the correctness boundary: even if an expired id still sits in the tray cache before TTL purge, the service drops any entry past its expiresAt before returning. The cache is never read by the client directly.

steps.title: 6. View Counts and Viewer Lists
description: Authors see who viewed their story (a viewer list) and a count. Views are high-volume; recording each synchronously to the story record would hotspot popular authors. The view service authenticates the request, checks the story is not expired and is visible to this viewer, marks seen, and emits a view event to a stream; a view aggregator consumes events and builds the per-story viewer list and count in a viewer store.

Dedup matters: mobile clients retry and the stream is at-least-once, so both the mark-seen write and the aggregator dedup by (viewer_id, story_id) — duplicate view events never inflate the unique viewer list or count. The mark-seen happens synchronously in the view service (so the UX updates immediately) while the viewer-list/count aggregation runs async off the hot path.

The viewer list is itself ephemeral — it only matters while the story is alive — so it expires with the story, again bounding the data. The viewer list is capped/paginated and ordered by first view (earliest viewer first), with recency available as a secondary sort. Counts are exact for ordinary stories but switch to an approximate sketch (HyperLogLog) for celebrity-scale stories where exactness doesn't matter and a unique-count estimate in fixed space is far cheaper.

The viewer list is author-only: GET /v1/stories/{id}/viewers goes through the view service, which verifies the caller owns the story before reading the viewer store — the store is never a client-addressable surface, mirroring the seen-state and tray boundaries.

view.caption: The client records a view through the view service, which marks seen idempotently and emits an event; the aggregator consumes events and builds the per-story viewer list and count in the viewer store.

options.name: Async view-event stream + aggregator (default)
options.description: View events are emitted to a stream and an aggregator builds the per-story viewer list and count off the hot path, also driving seen-state.
pros: View events go to a stream; an aggregator builds the per-story viewer list and count off the hot path.
A celebrity's 10M views never hotspot the story row with synchronous writes.
The same events drive seen-state, so capture and aggregation share one pipeline.
cons: Counts are eventually consistent — the displayed number lags real-time by the aggregation window.
A stream + aggregator to operate and monitor for lag.
Viewer list must be capped/paginated and expired with the story.

view.caption: The view service marks seen and emits events to the stream; an aggregator builds the per-story viewer list and count off the hot path, all TTL-aligned to the story.

options.name: Synchronous counter on the story row
options.description: Each view synchronously increments a counter column on the story record so the count is exact and immediately up to date.
pros: View count is exact and instantly up to date on every read.
Simplest model: increment a column when a view happens.
No stream or aggregator to build.
cons: A celebrity's story row becomes a write hotspot under millions of concurrent view increments.
Contention on that single row throttles the whole popular-author path.
Capturing a full viewer list synchronously is even worse at this volume.

view.caption: The client synchronously increments a view counter on the story row in the story store.

options.name: Approximate counters (sketch / sampling)
options.description: A probabilistic counter such as HyperLogLog estimates unique views in tiny fixed space, cheap to update at celebrity-scale volume.
pros: A probabilistic counter (e.g. HyperLogLog) gives unique-view counts in tiny, fixed space.
Cheap to update at massive volume — ideal for celebrity stories where exactness doesn't matter.
Bounded memory regardless of how viral a story goes.
cons: Counts are approximate, with a small error band — unacceptable if exactness is promised.
Doesn't yield an exact viewer list, only a count estimate.
Two regimes (exact for small, approximate for huge) add a branch to maintain.

view.caption: View events feed a view event stream that updates an approximate counter (HLL sketch) instead of an exact count.

steps.decisionPrompt: A celebrity's story gets 10M views. Do you write each view to the story row?
whyNow: View tracking is a core story feature and a high-volume counter; async aggregation off the hot path is the standard scaling answer.

concepts.definition: Aggregate view events into per-story (TTL'd) viewer lists/counts off the hot path.
concepts.whyItMatters: Avoids hotspotting popular stories; the data self-bounds with the story.
concepts.example: View events -> aggregate -> viewer list (expires with the story).

recap.newRisk: Posting spikes, big fanouts, and tray reads must scale; nothing should linger.

flows.name: View event → async viewer list + seen-state
flows.note: The view service marks seen synchronously (idempotent) and emits an event; viewer-list/count aggregation runs async off the hot path, deduped by (viewer, story) so popular stories don't hotspot or double-count.

steps.title: 7. Scaling and Cleanup at Volume
description: Shard stories by author and trays/seen-state by viewer; replicate tray caches for read volume. Media is CDN-served, absorbing view fanout. Fanout bursts (a popular account posts) buffer in a queue drained by scalable workers — bounded async fanout — so a spike delays tray landing by seconds rather than dropping work or stalling the post path; celebrity authors use the pull path to avoid mega-fanout. Queue lag is a first-class product signal: if it grows, some followers see the story appear late.

The ephemerality is a scaling gift: total live data is bounded to ~24h of stories, so stores never grow unbounded and TTL purge keeps them lean automatically — no ballooning storage, no giant delete jobs.

Treat trays, seen-state, and viewer lists as rebuildable derived state (rebuild a tray from followees' active stories on cache loss). The story store + media are the authoritative, self-expiring truth. Rebuilds are rate-limited so a mass tray-cache eviction doesn't stampede the story store with simultaneous fan-in queries.

Operate on a handful of signals: fanout queue lag, tray read p99, stale-filter drop rate, TTL purge lag, view aggregation lag, and CDN expired-media (404/expired) rate. These, not the happy path, are what tell you the ephemeral guarantees are actually holding under load.

view.caption: Posts persist to the sharded story store and enqueue fanout; workers drain the queue and push into tray caches; the CDN fronts the object store for media; observability tracks queue lag, tray p99, and stale-filter drops.

options.name: Rate-limited rebuild + serve partial tray (default)
options.description: On a tray-cache shard loss, rebuild each tray from followees' currently-active stories, but throttle/jitter the fan-in so the story store isn't stampeded, and serve a partial (best-effort) tray while it warms.
pros: The story store (source of truth) is protected by a bounded rebuild rate, so a mass eviction can't take it down.
Users keep getting a usable tray during recovery instead of an error.
Reuses the rebuildable-derived-state model — no extra durable storage for trays.
cons: During the warm-up window some users see an incomplete tray (a few stories missing until rebuilt).
Rebuild throttling adds a tuning knob (rate, jitter, priority) to operate.
A cold shard takes time to fully repopulate under the rate cap.

view.caption: On cache loss the tray read service rebuilds from followees' active stories via the graph and story store, rate-limited, serving a partial tray while warming.

options.name: Block reads until the tray is fully rebuilt
options.description: Treat the tray as authoritative: on cache loss, fully reconstruct it before serving any read, so users never see a partial tray.
pros: Every served tray is complete — no transient missing-story window.
Simple correctness story: a read only succeeds against a fully warm tray.
cons: Reads fail or hang during rebuild — a tray-cache outage becomes a user-visible read outage.
A mass eviction triggers synchronized full rebuilds, exactly the story-store stampede to avoid.
Worst behavior happens precisely when load is already high.

view.caption: Reads block while the tray read service fully reconstructs the tray from the story store and graph before serving.

options.name: Make tray cache durable (replicate / persist)
options.description: Avoid rebuilds entirely by treating the tray cache as a durable, replicated store so a node loss never loses tray state.
pros: No rebuild path and no story-store fan-in on node loss.
Tray reads stay complete and fast through failures.
cons: Pays durability/replication cost for state that is cheap to recompute and expires in 24h anyway.
Contradicts the derived-state model: now there are two sources of truth to keep consistent.
Higher steady-state cost for a transient-data store.

view.caption: The tray cache is replicated/persisted so a node loss never needs a rebuild from the story store.

steps.decisionPrompt: A tray-cache shard is lost during peak traffic. What do you serve while it rebuilds — a partial tray, nothing, or do you make the cache durable so it never happens?
whyNow: Closing on sharding + bounded live data shows the ephemerality is a scaling advantage and the derived stores are rebuildable — completing the design.

concepts.definition: Buffer fanout jobs in a queue drained by autoscaled workers, with idempotent tray writes and a lag budget; shed nothing, just absorb bursts.
concepts.whyItMatters: A viral post becomes seconds of queue lag instead of a write storm that stalls the post path or drops deliveries.
concepts.example: A celebrity-adjacent author with 2M followers posts; the queue lag rises to ~8s and drains, rather than 2M synchronous tray writes blocking the post.

concepts.definition: Treat trays, seen-state, and viewer lists as caches that can be rebuilt from the authoritative, self-expiring story store; rate-limit rebuilds.
concepts.whyItMatters: Cache loss is recoverable and never corrupts the source of truth; rate limiting prevents a rebuild stampede.
concepts.example: A lost tray shard rebuilds from followees' currently-active stories, throttled so the story store isn't hit by every viewer at once.

recap.after: Sharded, CDN-served, bounded-live-data, rebuildable trays, queued fanout, operating signals.
recap.newRisk: Fanout lag on viral posts and cache rebuilds — bounded by queueing, pull, rate-limited rebuilds, and observability.

traps.trap: Treating TTL purge as exact, instant deletion everywhere.
traps.why: Purge timing is approximate and backups/replicas/CDN caches/logs lag; conflating purge with the guarantee leaks stale stories.
traps.instead: Make read-time filtering and capped media-URL validity the guarantee; let purge be approximate cleanup.

traps.trap: Letting the client clock decide whether a story is expired.
traps.why: Client clocks skew and are spoofable, so a viewer could see content past expiry.
traps.instead: Server assigns expiresAt at create time; reads filter against the server's authoritative expiry.

traps.trap: Rebuilding every evicted tray immediately on a cache outage.
traps.why: A mass eviction triggers simultaneous fan-in queries that stampede the story store.
traps.instead: Rate-limit/jitter rebuilds and serve a partial tray while it warms.

bottlenecks.issue: Big fanout on a popular post.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Queue + autoscaled fanout workers with idempotent tray writes; pull for celebrity authors; alert on queue lag.
Rebuild from followees' active (unexpired) stories, rate-limited to avoid a story-store stampede.

bottlenecks.issue: View aggregation lag during a celebrity spike.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Partition the view stream by story; scale aggregator consumers; fall back to HLL count estimates.

failureDrills.scenario: Fanout queue lag spikes after a popular author posts.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The post still returns immediately; workers autoscale and drain; affected followers see the story land a few seconds late, within the lag budget.
failureDrills.mitigation: Bounded async fanout with a queue, autoscaled workers, idempotent tray writes, and a queue-lag alert; celebrities use pull so they never enqueue mega-fanout.

failureDrills.scenario: A tray cache shard is evicted and many users rebuild at once.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Rebuild pulls followees' currently-active stories; only unexpired ones appear; rebuilds are throttled so the story store isn't stampeded.
failureDrills.mitigation: Tray as rebuildable derived state over the authoritative store, with rate-limited/jittered rebuilds and a partial tray served while warming.

failureDrills.scenario: The CDN serves a media object after the story expired.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The signed URL's validity is capped at the story expiry, so the fetch is rejected even if the object lingers in an edge cache.
failureDrills.mitigation: Cap signed-URL / CDN-authorization TTL to story expiry; monitor CDN expired-media (404/expired) rate.

failureDrills.scenario: A story is reported or policy-blocked and must disappear before its 24h TTL.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The story's status flips to taken_down: read paths treat it like an expired entry (the tray read service and view service drop it on read, so it vanishes from trays and can't be viewed), media URLs are revoked / CDN entries invalidated so the blob can't be fetched, and the viewer list stops growing. Cached tray responses age out within their short TTL or are invalidated. Physical media deletion still follows the same approximate-purge path as expiry — the user-facing guarantee is enforced at read time, not by instant erasure.
failureDrills.mitigation: Treat takedown as early expiry: set status=taken_down (an authoritative read-time gate alongside expiresAt), revoke signed URLs / invalidate CDN, and let TTL/lifecycle reclaim bytes. The safety scan in Step 2 blocks unsafe media before it ever goes live; takedown handles content reported after it does.

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Ephemeral Stories
finalDesign.description: Stories are TTL-native end to end: media (object lifecycle rule) and story records (DB TTL) auto-expire, so cleanup is automatic and cheap. Posting durably writes the story and enqueues fanout; workers drain the queue and push a story id (idempotently) into eligible followers' tray caches (hybrid: pull for celebrities; audience checks at fanout time), with tray entries carrying the story's TTL. Reads serve the tray with per-viewer seen-state (unseen-first) and hydrate media from the CDN via signed URLs capped to the story expiry, always filtering expired entries on read so no stale story is ever shown (a reaper is only a cheap backstop). The view service marks seen idempotently and emits events; an aggregator builds ephemeral, capped viewer lists and counts (HLL at celebrity scale) in the viewer store. Stores shard by author/viewer; total live data is bounded to ~24h; trays/seen-state/viewer-lists are rebuildable derived state (rate-limited rebuilds) over the self-expiring authoritative story store, with observability on queue lag, tray p99, stale-filter drops, and view aggregation lag.

view.caption: The client posts through the gateway to the post service, which stores media (served via the CDN) and stories, then enqueues fanout (workers push ids into tray caches via the social graph). Reads go through the tray read service (expiry filter, seen-state, audience revalidation, celebrity merge); the view service owns mark-seen, view events flow to async aggregation, and author-authorized viewer-list reads come back through the view service; observability watches the operating signals.

functional.how: View service emits events; aggregator builds capped, TTL'd viewer lists/counts (HLL at celebrity scale) in the viewer store.

Story carries audience + snapshot version; checked at fanout time (only eligible followers' trays) and revalidated at read time (tray read + view recording) against current relationship state, so an unfollow/block/close-friends removal during the 24h window suppresses the entry.
The view service verifies the caller owns the story before returning the viewer list/count from the viewer store.

nonFunctional.how: ~24h live data + sharding + rebuildable derived state.

tradeoff: Native per-row/item TTL removes the need for a scheduled delete job, but purge timing is approximate.
Self-hosting gives control over compaction/TTL behavior at the cost of operating the cluster.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed TTL store removes the need for a hand-built reaper as the primary deleter — the reaper is only a backstop.

tradeoff: An in-memory cache gives low-latency tray reads but is derived state that must be rebuildable.
TTL-aligned entries let the tray self-clean instead of needing explicit deletes.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Rebuildable derived state means cache durability is not required — a lost tray is reconstructed from the story store.

tradeoff: A partitioned log (Kafka/Kinesis) gives ordered, replayable, high-throughput view events; a queue (SQS) is simpler for fanout jobs.
At-least-once delivery requires downstream dedup by (viewer, story).
Object lifecycle rules auto-delete expired media, but lifecycle runs on a coarse schedule — cap signed-URL TTL to story expiry so stale media can't be fetched.
A CDN absorbs read fanout but can cache media past expiry without a bounded URL.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Object lifecycle rules remove the need to delete media blobs manually.

tradeoff: Exact counts/sets are fine for ordinary stories; HLL gives unique-count estimates in fixed space for celebrity-scale views.
A capped, paginated viewer list bounds memory regardless of virality.

say: Confirm ephemeral: stories disappear after ~24h, read-heavy trays.
Pin reliable+cheap expiry and never-show-expired as the goals.
Note seen-state per viewer and the heavy-tailed follower distribution.
Fanout into per-user trays (hybrid for celebrities).
Reliable expiry: server-assigned expiresAt + filter-on-read + capped media-URL TTL.
Async view counting / viewer lists (view service → stream → aggregator, deduped, ephemeral).
Scaling: bounded live data, sharding, queued (bounded) fanout, rebuildable trays.
Tradeoffs: push vs pull threshold, purge lag vs read-filter, view-count accuracy.
Mention close-friends/visibility and replies as extensions.

expectations: Stores stories with a TTL and fans out to trays.
May rely on a delete job and miss read-filtering/seen-state.
Reasons that ephemerality bounds data + seen-state.
Handles celebrity fanout and rebuildable trays at scale.
Treats audience as a contract over the live window: fanout-time eligibility plus read-time revalidation for unfollow/block/close-friends changes, and author-only viewer-list access.
Handles pre-expiry takedown as early expiry through the same read-time gate.

followUps: How do you guarantee an expired story is never shown even before TTL purge runs?
How would you support visibility controls (close friends, hidden-from lists)?
How do you bound per-viewer seen-state from growing unbounded?
How would you handle replies/reactions to a story (DMs)?
Why is an ephemeral feed easier to scale than a permanent one?
